text: The catalytic conversion of CO2 into renewable fuels like methanol is a highly sought after strategy for mitigation of climate change. However, this process is plagued by the high reactivity of carbon, which is often compensated by the use of harsh conditions, including elevated temperatures and pressures. The use of bulk gold for hydrogenation reactions is also met with inactivity as the chemical inertness of gold has proven to be insurmountable. However, our study demonstrated the exceptional reactivity and selectivity of gold nanoparticles supported on a CeOx/TiO2(110) interface for methanol synthesis at low pressures. Utilizing ambient-pressure X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (AP-XPS) and scanning tunneling microscopy (STM), the addition of ceria nanoclusters to a titania support is shown to dramatically enhance the dispersion of gold at the surface of the support and create sites with high reactivity. This is due to electronic metal-support interaction originating from local charge redistribution within the Au nanoparticles upon adsorption of reactants and the stabilization of high-reactivity carboxylate and formate intermediates. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations that investigated the different possible sites available to CO2 on Au/TiO2 show that that Ce3+ sites present in the mixed oxide support lead to a significantly reduced energy barrier associated with CO2 activation as well as the subsequent hydrogenation steps as compared to Au/TiO2. Thus, a reducible mixed-oxide interface can transform an inert noble metal catalyst into a highly reactive one upon strong interactions.
